title: A Father Who Gave Us a World
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier for the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian NMAAHC in Columbus, Georgia, in April 2011, Ruby Sales opens with her father, the son of a formerly enslaved teacher and an itinerant Baptist preacher, an Army chaplain radicalized by serving in a segregated military who taught his children about Port Chicago and asked why Black men died abroad but could not vote at home, and her mother, who would confront any white person who disrespected her in a downtown store.

title: A Sales, Not a Fletcher
summary: Sales describes the town where her grandmother lived, where she estimates thirty percent of the people were Sales relatives and generations of the family lay in the cemetery, and how being told she was just like her aunt Nen or aunt Rae made her see herself all her life as a Sales in temperament, looks, and love of knowledge rather than a Fletcher, her mother's family.

title: Truman's Army and Childish Rebellions
summary: She recalls that because of Truman's integration of the Army, her family near Fort Benning could ride the front of the bus and use integrated theaters, restaurants, and hospitals, an experience not replicated in civil society, and how she and her friends intuitively challenged segregation by trying on bathing suits in white stores and ringing the bus buzzer before running off laughing.

title: A Training Ground for Citizenship
summary: Sales recounts moving from a Catholic school to Carver Elementary and Carver High in the Carver Heights subdivision built on FHA loans for military families, where teachers under principal S.P. Charleston insulated students from segregation so they would not be broken-wing birds and connected their education to the project of freedom and the advancement of the whole community.

title: A Lot of Mouth for a Young Girl
summary: She describes herself as a cheerleader, newspaper editor, and Honor Society member who was a well-known rebel allowed to challenge rules because her powerful home teacher saw something worth cultivating and her teachers understood rebellion as a substance of survival, not wanting students to accommodate segregation or get killed.

title: Going to Tuskegee at Sixteen
summary: Sales explains that a regional program let students who passed a test enter college early, so at sixteen she followed her home teacher Marian Armstrong to Tuskegee Institute in the fall of 1963, after abandoned fantasies of joining the Army and attending Drake, and finds a campus already charged with movement consciousness through Gwen Patton and Professor Charles Gomillion's challenge to gerrymandering.

title: No Chariot, No God
topic: Galvanized and Locked in at the Capitol
summary: Sales recalls wanting to be a beatnik who quoted Allen Ginsberg until Stokely Carmichael and people from Lowndes County came to campus and galvanized her, so that she threw away her guitar pick and grew her hair natural, and describes the Tuskegee student demonstration over the Edmund Pettus Bridge beatings, being locked in at the Montgomery Capitol amid mounted men with billy clubs, and how, waiting in vain for a delivering God, she lost her Southern Baptist religion while watching a SNCC woman face down a club-raising officer.

title: Sleeping in the Same Room
topic: Breaking Boundaries at the Freedom House
summary: She recounts her home teacher Marian Armstrong driving eighty miles to ask her to leave the church, a request she refused as a defining break from older adults' approval, and how moving into a freedom house meant sleeping in the same room as men without sex, redefining the relationship between men and women as comradeship and friendship.

title: Stunned, Not Angry
summary: Sales reflects on her amazement that movement-supporting church deacons could lock the students out, deny them food, and want them gone, on Stokely Carmichael's nervous breakdown in the Montgomery airport from years under siege, and on why she held no grudge, reared to question elders without harboring deep hate.

title: Rethinking What a Woman Is
summary: She describes the profound shift in how she saw herself as a young woman, questioning whether she had to marry and have children, and how working alongside men and depending on them led her to relate to them through friendship and trust rather than reducing them to sexual objects.

title: There Was Always My Mouth
summary: Sales answers why she was willing to go into Lowndes County while others were not, crediting parents who spoke out against injustice, her principal's prediction that her big mouth would make her a great civil rights lawyer, her rebellious search for larger meaning, and a self-image, as a champion marble player who beat boys at basketball, that refused the small circumscribed space reserved for women.

title: An Integrated Hell Tonight
summary: She recalls her first day registering voters when a sheriff drew a gun on Stokely Carmichael and threatened to blow his brains out, Carmichael answering that hell would be an integrated hell that night, the stories of violent Bloody Lowndes told by local people, and the bravery and hospitality of residents like Mr. Jackson, who gave them a freedom house, and Clara Maul, who risked her life driving them.

title: Ordinary People, Extraordinary Things
summary: Sales describes a typical day in Lowndes County knocking on doors and seeing extreme poverty, hungry shoeless children, and people picking cotton and cucumbers, and how she measured progress not by her own work but by watching local people execute leadership in mass meetings, organize neighbors, and refuse to be intimidated, developing a lifelong respect for ordinary people doing extraordinary things.

title: He Showed Up Every Time
summary: She gives portraits of John Hulett, the first uneducated man she had seen in a leadership position, whom she trusted because he kept his word in violent circumstances and never hogged the microphone, and Clara Maul, a married mother who drove a hundred miles an hour and, protected because her grandmother owned a bar and had a liaison with a rich white man, could move through the county freely and was relied on even by Stokely.

title: How Do You Squelch Their Spirit
topic: The SNCC Debate Over Fort Deposit
summary: Around the August 1965 signing of the Voting Rights Act, Sales recounts the young people of Fort Deposit, angry that their sharecropping parents were cheated at the plantation store, bringing SNCC a plan to demonstrate, and the hours-long debate in which she argued the action was too dangerous while Stokely Carmichael and Jonathan Daniels argued to keep the movement open to all, a democratizing vision that won her over.

title: A Garbage Truck to Hayneville
topic: The Mob, the Jail, and Release
summary: She describes the Saturday demonstration confronted by a mob of white men armed with baseball bats and anything they could grab, the arrest she believes also spared their lives, being loaded onto a garbage truck and taken to the barbaric Hayneville jail where big-mouthed women were threatened with rape, her developing an ulcer while begging to see a doctor, and the jailer suddenly turning them loose, which terrified her because the same had happened to Goodman and Chaney.

title: Bitch, I'll Blow Your Brains Out
summary: Sales recounts walking with the group to a corner store after release, a later account by Willie Vaughn that an armed man waited there, and Tom Coleman appearing in the doorway and aiming at her in front before she was pulled backward and heard the shot that killed Jonathan Daniels instantly and a second that wounded Father Richard Morrisroe, who lay moaning for water; hearing Joyce Bailey call her name, she realized she was alive and crawled away while Coleman flung his gun and threatened to kill anyone who helped the priest.

title: On Top of Jonathan's Body
summary: She recounts Stokely Carmichael and Silas Norman finding the downtown streets cleaned and Daniels and Morrisroe missing, several days of Ruby Doris Smith Robinson and the SNCC office unable to learn their whereabouts even through John Doar of the Justice Department, and the discovery that Morrisroe had been carried to the hospital in a hearse on top of Daniels's dead body and left in the hallway for hours.

title: From Big Mouth to Silence
summary: Sales reflects that none of the young workers, none over twenty-two, had faced such Southern white murder before, how the group was shattered yet felt responsible for one another and dreaded facing Daniels's mother at the funeral, and how she went from a big-mouth person to one who rarely spoke, spending much of her adult life, even at Princeton and Manhattanville, never telling anyone she had been in the movement.

title: If You Testify, They Will Kill You
summary: She describes returning home to supportive parents, white men coming to the backyard while her mother hung wash to warn that she would be killed if she testified, her mother who never told her not to testify, and John Doar's words that the wheels of justice move very slowly.

title: Sleeping With His Whore
summary: Sales recounts her determination to testify and meet the defense lawyer word for word in a courtroom where hostility hung like icicles, and how the lawyer, said to be one of the greatest in Alabama history, insinuated she was a whore and that Daniels, like white men in the South, was sleeping with her, trying to build the case on the South's unspoken truth, though she and Daniels had no such relationship.

title: The Commitment Was Solidified
summary: Asked whether the trauma made her reconsider nonviolence, Sales reaffirms that she never considered anything else, finding killing repugnant even as part of the movement took up arms, and explains that the killing did not defeat the Lowndes County movement, where people who had lived with white violence for generations were bound together by collective grief over Daniels and grew more determined to move forward.

title: The Second Indigenous Movement
summary: She describes Tent City in 1966, sharecroppers evicted for registering to vote who chose to live in tents rather than be controlled by economic penalties, which she frames as part of the second indigenous Southern freedom movement after the runaway slave movement, a vision rooted in the will of the people that fed the later Washington Tent City under Abernathy, the Black Panther symbol, and an effort to build a party improving on the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party.

title: The Girl That Jonathan Saved
summary: Sales recounts the years after Lowndes County: a speaking tour, a brief stint in Albany working with Charles Sherrod, resettling in Washington with Gene Wiley, living in James Baldwin's apartment in New York while he was away, becoming involved in the feminist movement and a global consciousness, and wrestling with how to carry the label of the girl Jonathan saved while building a life beyond it.

title: Not Civil Rights, but Freedom
topic: Why It Was a Freedom Movement
summary: Sales explains why she calls it the Southern Freedom Movement rather than the civil rights movement: it sought human dignity and an end to a century of white terrorism, larger creative and political space, and benefits for white people too, and was a dynamic process connected to earlier efforts like the Southern Negro Youth Cooperative, while pointing out that Rosa Parks's act was also a rebellion of working-class maids assaulted on Montgomery buses.

title: Out of the Ashes of Segregation
summary: Drawing on her graduate study, Sales critiques narratives dominated by whites that assumed Black people had second-class minds, ignored the network Black educators built across the South as described by Vanessa Siddle Walker, and reduced Black life to deficit and pathology rather than what, as Ralph Ellison said, was made out of the ashes of segregation, and notes that historians credited Crozer and Boston University for King while missing the cultural resources he drew on to testify with the people.

title: A Culture of Intimacy
summary: She argues the movement happened because of a shared Southern intimacy that historians overlook, a closed society bound by blood and common experience, where Bernice Reagon brought what she had learned as a Southern Black girl, where people sang the same songs and prayed the same prayers, stayed with one another because hotels were closed to them, and where the playground was an unrecognized training ground for young people.

title: Snake, Snake, and Enslavement
summary: Sales explains two deliberate language choices: refusing the defense lawyer's familiar pronunciation of SNCC because he lacked the intimacy to claim that territory, and as a history major preferring enslavement over slavery and enslaver over master, terms that name the perpetrator and the brutality of captivity rather than implying passive submission or a benign Atlantic passage, insisting that history make white men the visible subjects responsible for their deeds.

title: Stilling History
summary: Returning to Lowndes County in about 2005 for public events few whites attended, Sales describes how those in power maintain it through dismembering and revisionist history that wipes their hands of the blood, makes the oppressor the good guy, and feeds a post-racial myth, and she ties this to cultural wars that turned liberation theology into prosperity gospel, tore down Black schools as David Cecelski documents, and fired tens of thousands of Black teachers across Southern states.

title: A New Plantation System
summary: Sales argues that Black people did not endure a century through the veil of segregation merely for one seat in the White House but to advance democracy for all, and that a revisionist climate criminalizing Black men opened the door to a prison industrial complex, a new plantation system where young men work in jail for corporations, while Southern culture's materialism and anti-intellectualism, exported north through a tax on liberalism, came to hold both worlds together.

title: The Cultural Wars They Won
summary: She contends that the South lost the freedom-movement war but won the cultural wars, so that by the 1980s Northern and Southern politicians were galvanized around the same whiteness, and frames the twenty-first century as an anti-human technocracy in which only a few matter and the rest, including obsolete white males who blame others, are treated as collateral damage and human waste, a fascist drift she traces to the heartbeat of Southern politics and the destruction of the New Deal's cap on corporate greed.

title: How We Live in Relation to Each Other
summary: Sales closes by describing Spirit House, named for breath and the integration of inner conviction with outward living in relation to one another, which brings people of all colors together on education, economics, and racial justice, and for two years has studied the segregation-era counterculture of education to learn from the Southern Black teachers who made outstanding students, work she connects to the survival of HBCUs being folded into struggling white colleges, before the interview closes with thanks.
